Heat Flow Near Arcs

Spatial Continuity and Sampling Strategies

Buchanan Kerswell

12/15/2020

Shameless Plug

My Website My Resume

Enjoy this short presentation. :)

Introduction

Subduction zones are recognized as important features of an active planet. Foundering oceanic lithosphere drives opening and closing of ocean basins (Wilson, 1966), orogenesis, arc volcanism, deadly megathrust earthquakes, and is the primary mechanism for planet-scale chemical differentiation (Grove et al., 2009; Hacker et al., 2003; McKenzie, 1969; S. A. Peacock, 1990; S. M. Peacock, 1996). However, these processes operate at depths of 10\(^1\) to 10\(^2\) kilometers and at rates of a few cm/year. To understand subduction, we must rely on seismic imaging of modern subduction zones (Currie & Hyndman, 2006; Engdahl et al., 1998), records of ancient subduction zones preserved in exhumed rocks (Agard et al., 2009; Penniston-Dorland et al., 2015), and numerical models (e.g., Gerya et al., 2008). With the exception of numerical models, these datasets are expensive to collect, sparse, discontinuous across time and space, and layered with complexity.

Previous work demonstrates how strengths of brittle faults (Gao & Wang, 2014), depths of seismic and mechanical coupling (Furukawa, 1993; Kerswell et al., 2020; Wada & Wang, 2009), and thermal structure of subduction zones (Currie & Hyndman, 2006) can be inverted from surface heat flow (e.g. Figure 1). This study focused on analyzing the spatial distribution and continuity of the heat flow near arcs to assess the reliability of inversion techniques. We sampled the global heat flow database (Lucazeau, 2019) within 1000 km of thirteen arc segments and used kriging to provide a continuous estimation of heat flow. The kriged results show that previous sampling methods may have overestimated the spatial continuity of heat flow near arcs. We caution against extrapolation of surface heat flow inversions within, and among arc segments. Inferences derived from paterns of heat flow should be considered representative of local phenomenon only.

Figure 1. Sampling surface heat flow along a transect perpendicular to the Sumatra arc segment. Consistently high heat flow in the backarc is interpreted to represent a thin (warm) backarc lithosphere. From Currie & Hyndman (2006).

Methods

The global heat flow dataset contains over 60000 individual terrestrial and marine heat flow measurements (Lucazeau, 2019). The dataset can be downloaded from the International Heat Flow Commission’s website. The database is under revision and currently has no quality control.

The dataset was first filtered to gather measurements within a 1000 km buffer for each arc segment (Figure 2). Segment geometries are from Syracuse & Abers (2006). The data were then filtered to remove measurements with:

  1. Negative thermal gradients
  2. Heat flow below 10 \(mW/m^2\)
  3. Heat flow above 120 \(mW/m^2\)
  4. Thermal gradients above 500 \(mK/m\)
Table 1. Filtered heat flow measurements within 1000 km of arc segments
segment n
Kamchatka Marianas 824
Kyushu Ryukyu 499
Sumatra Banda Sea 446
Central America 303
Andes 237
Tonga New Zealand 226
N. Philippines 143
S. Philippines 105
New Britain Solomon 73
Lesser Antilles 68
Alaska Aleutians 66
Vanuatu 21
Scotia 11

Our filtering scheme follows Currie & Hyndman (2006). We attempted basic quality control and removal of measurements that represent circulation of seawater within seafloor sediments and crust. Our goal was to “see through” near-surface processes that would obfuscate direct correspondence between surface heat flow and subduction zone thermal structure. A summary of the filtered dataset is given in Table 1 and Figure 3.

Figure 3. Filtered heat flow data within 1000 km of arc segments. The data are broadly distributed within, and among, arc segments.

Experimental variograms, variogram model fitting, kriging, and interpolation were achieved with a one-line function (see Appendix) that wraps functions from the R geostat package (Gräler et al., 2016; Pebesma, 2004). One-hundred lags were used to capture the variability in the experimental variogram and spherical variogram models were fit in all cases. Segments with median number of point-pairs less than thirty were not included in the analysis. Ordinary kriging and a inverse distance weighted scheme was used for interpolation.

Results

Seven out of thirteen arc segments have sufficient heat flow data to calculate experimental variograms with a median number of point-pairs greater than thirty (Table 2).

Table 2. Summary of point-pairs in experimental variograms
segment np.max np.min np.mean np.median
Kamchatka Marianas 9134 1153 2207 1922
Kyushu Ryukyu 1553 269 665 608
Sumatra Banda Sea 1078 354 606 587
Central America 935 124 334 302
Andes 831 46 160 134
Tonga New Zealand 522 1 181 106
N. Philippines 168 17 61 46
S. Philippines 100 11 29 26
Alaska Aleutians 33 3 12 11
Lesser Antilles 24 1 11 10
New Britain Solomon 102 1 18 8
Scotia 2 1 1 1
Vanuatu 4 1 2 1

Experimental variograms with less than thirty point pairs per lag are suspect, so only Kamchatka-Marianas, Kyushu-Ryukyu, Sumatra-Banda Sea, Tonga-New Zealand, N. Philippines, Andes, and Central America are considered in the Kriging analysis. Five are presented below for brevity.

Table 3. Summary of modelled variogram parameter estimates
segment nugget sill range
Kamchatka Marianas 497 752 51
Kyushu Ryukyu 376 545 6
Sumatra Banda Sea 89 987 1707
Central America 53 435 36
Andes 189 766 29
Tonga New Zealand 376 1638 4734
N. Philippines 9 138 16

The modelled variogram parameter estimates vary from segment to segment (Table 3 & Figure 4). The modelled variogram ranges range from <10\(^1\) km (Kyushu-Ryukyu) to >10\(^2\) km. The range in nuggets and sills is less important for assessing spatial correlation, but also range widely from segment to segment.

Figure 4. Modelled variogram parameter estimates. Huge variability in the ranges among segment is difficult to interpret, but indicates a wide range of spatial continuity with respect to surface heat flow.

Figure 5. Kriging results for Kamchatka-Marianas. a) The experimental (dots) and modelled (line) variograms. A spherical variogram model is used with parameters given in Table 3. The narrow modelled variogram range indicates low spatial correlation, except for points spaced less than ~51 km apart. b) Histogram shows an approximately normal distribution, but gives no spatial context. c) Interpolated heat flow generally conforms to the expectation of low heat flow in the forearc and high heat flow near the arc and into the backarc.

Figure 6. Kriging results for Kyushu-Ryukyu. a) The experimental (dots) and modelled (line) variograms. A spherical variogram model is used with parameters given in Table 3. The narrow modelled variogram range indicates low spatial correlation, except for points spaced less than ~6 km apart. b) Histogram shows an approximately normal distribution, but gives no spatial context. c) Interpolated heat flow shows a quasi-random distribution.

Figure 7. Kriging results for Sumatra-Banda Sea. a) The experimental (dots) and modelled (line) variograms. The high variance at a lag distance of 250 km may indicate some physical phenomenon operating on this scale. A spherical variogram model is used with parameters given in Table 3. The broad modelled variogram range indicates spatial correlation for points spaced up to ~750 km. b) Histogram shows bimodal distribution, but gives no spatial context. c) Interpolated heat flow is difficult to interpret because the heat flow data is clustered in near the arc to the west, and in the forearc to the east. The heat flow appears to generally conform to expected low heat flow in the forearc and high heat flow in the backarc.

Figure 8. Kriging results for Tonga-New Zealand. a) The experimental (dots) and modelled (line) variograms. A spherical variogram model is used with parameters given in Table 3. The modelled variogram range indicates spatial correlation for points spaced up to ~250 km. b) Histogram shows an approximately normal distribution, but gives no spatial context. c) Interpolated heat flow is difficult to interpret because most of the data lie near the arc. Heat flow near 40-degrees south shows a quasi-random distribution.

Figure 9. Kriging results for Central America. a) The experimental (dots) and modelled (line) variograms. A spherical variogram model is used with parameters given in Table 3. The narrow modelled variogram range indicates low spatial correlation, except for points spaced less than ~35 km apart. b) Histogram shows a bimodal, or strongly left-skewed distribution, but gives no spatial context. c) Interpolated heat flow shows a transition from high-low-high heat flow along the forearc. Trench-perpendicular heat flow patterns are difficult to interpret from lack of data in the arc region.

Discussion

Previous work has adopted common sampling strategies, where a transect is drawn perpendicular to the trench at a single location and all data within a given distance of the transect is gathered and projected onto the transect line, and then averaged (e.g. Currie & Hyndman, 2006, see Figure 1). The location of the transect and size of the sampling rectangle is chosen arbitrarily, in our estimation, such that enough data is gathered to estimate uncertainty when averaging heat flow. However, our results show that spatial correlation is limited from several km to several 10’s of km at most (compare modelled variogram ranges in Table 3). Furthermore, no consistent patterns of heat flow are apparent along arc segments, nor perpendicular to trenches with the available data. This implies that a suite of transects chosen along any single arc segment will likely yield spurious results (consider a suite of transects taken from Figure 5).

While some arc segments (Sumatra-Banda Sea and Kamchatka-Marianas) have regions which support the conclusions of currie2006, Wada & Wang (2009), and Kerswell et al. (2020), choosing another transect 50 km away may yield wildly different results, and thus, wildly different interpretations. This conclusion extends to comparisons among arc segments. The dissimilarity of experimental variograms from segment to segment implies that any physical phenomena that can be deduced from inverting heat flow should be considered local phenomena. Alternative explanations include: 1) subduction zone thermal structure is more complicated and cannot be considered common among arc segments, 2) surface heat flow is only loosely representative of thermal structure, 3) the dataset is too sparse to draw broad conclusions from, or 4) a combination of two or more of these.

Conclusion

Much has been inferred about seismic and geodynamic processes in subduction zones by inverting surface heat flow (Currie & Hyndman, 2006; Furukawa, 1993; Gao & Wang, 2014; Kerswell et al., 2020; Wada & Wang, 2009). However, the kriging analysis presented here indicates that methods of sampling and averaging surface heat flow along individual segments is not a robust method for investigating potential commonalities within, or among, arc segments. Rather, the interpolated distribution of heat flow near arcs indicates that the thermal structure and seismic activities near arcs is less spatially continuous than previously thought, surface heat flow signals are too mixed or too obfuscated to interpret, not enough data exists to make broad claims about subduction zones, or any combination of the above. Future work may consider more local phenomena from surface heat flow inversions, or focus on increasing the spatial coverage of the currently available dataset.

Appendix

Functions

Kriging

# Kriging
krg <- function(data, lags = 50, lag.cutoff = 5, param, krg.shp, seg, contours, crs, ngrid = 3e5, grid.method = 'hexagonal', v.mod = 'Sph'){
  d <- data
  cutoff <- max(st_distance(data))/lag.cutoff %>% as.numeric()
  class(cutoff) <- 'numeric'
  b <- krg.shp
  s <- b %>% st_sample(size = ngrid, grid.method)
  v <- variogram(get(param)~1, locations = d, cutoff = cutoff, width = cutoff/lags)
  f <- fit.variogram(v, vgm(NA, 'Sph', NA, NA))
  v.m <- variogramLine(f, maxdist = max(v$dist))
  k <- krige(formula = get(param)~1, locations = d, newdata = s) %>% select(-var1.var)
  return(list(variogram = v, v.model = f, krg = k))
}

References

Agard, P., Yamato, P., Jolivet, L., & Burov, E. (2009). Exhumation of oceanic blueschists and eclogites in subduction zones: Timing and mechanisms. Earth-Science Reviews, 92(1-2), 53–79.
Currie, C. A., & Hyndman, R. D. (2006). The thermal structure of subduction zone back arcs. Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth, 111(B8).
Engdahl, E. R., Hilst, R. van der, & Buland, R. (1998). Global teleseismic earthquake relocation with improved travel times and procedures for depth determination. Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America, 88(3), 722–743.
Furukawa, Y. (1993). Depth of the decoupling plate interface and thermal structure under arcs. Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth, 98(B11), 20005–20013.
Gao, X., & Wang, K. (2014). Strength of stick-slip and creeping subduction megathrusts from heat flow observations. Science, 345(6200), 1038–1041.
Gerya, T. V., Connolly, J. A., & Yuen, D. A. (2008). Why is terrestrial subduction one-sided? Geology, 36(1), 43–46.
Gräler, B., Pebesma, E., & Heuvelink, G. (2016). Spatio-temporal interpolation using gstat. The R Journal, 8, 204–218. Retrieved from https://journal.r-project.org/archive/2016/RJ-2016-014/index.html
Grove, T., Till, C., Lev, E., Chatterjee, N., & Médard, E. (2009). Kinematic variables and water transport control the formation and location of arc volcanoes. Nature, 459(7247), 694–697.
Hacker, B. R., Abers, G. A., & Peacock, S. M. (2003). Subduction factory 1. Theoretical mineralogy, densities, seismic wave speeds, and H2O contents. Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth, 108(B1).
Kerswell, B. C., Kohn, M. J., & Gerya, T. V. (2020). Backarc lithospheric thickness and serpentine stability control slab-mantle coupling depths in subduction zones. Earth and Space Science Open Archive, 34. https://doi.org/10.1002/essoar.10503710.1
Lucazeau, F. (2019). Analysis and mapping of an updated terrestrial heat flow data set. Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems, 20(8), 4001–4024.
McKenzie, D. P. (1969). Speculations on the consequences and causes of plate motions. Geophysical Journal International, 18(1), 1–32.
Peacock, S. A. (1990). Fluid processes in subduction zones. Science, 248(4953), 329–337.
Peacock, S. M. (1996). Thermal and petrologic structure of subduction zones. Subduction: Top to Bottom, 96, 119–133.
Pebesma, E. J. (2004). Multivariable geostatistics in S: The gstat package. Computers & Geosciences, 30, 683–691.
Penniston-Dorland, S. C., Kohn, M. J., & Manning, C. E. (2015). The global range of subduction zone thermal structures from exhumed blueschists and eclogites: Rocks are hotter than models. Earth and Planetary Science Letters, 428, 243–254.
Syracuse, E. M., & Abers, G. A. (2006). Global compilation of variations in slab depth beneath arc volcanoes and implications. Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems, 7(5).
Wada, I., & Wang, K. (2009). Common depth of slab-mantle decoupling: Reconciling diversity and uniformity of subduction zones. Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems, 10(10).
Wilson, J. T. (1966). Did the atlantic close and then re-open? Nature, 211(5050), 676–681.
